speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, January 2, 1996, tape number five, survivor, David Klebanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, David, you told me you found out your wife had died. Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife stayed all the time in Stutthof

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she die? From what?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- when the Russians came, and they had to leave Stutthof, they were put on a ship. And with this ship, went to-- through the Baltic Sea to the west. And the guards were terrible guards. They didn't give them soft water, you know. Water-- they had to drink from the sea. My wife finally-- they left. People went to this ship, found-- I don't know how many-- 30, 40 Jewish woman, 50. I don't know how many.

speaker: interviewer
text: They died.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My wife came. She was sick. She was taken to the hospital in a Eckernforde, German hospital. She was diagnosed. She has TB of the lungs. TB of the lungs, with the people in the concentration camp, was-- right away you're pronounced dead-- pronounced dead-- dead. Nobody survived under [? their ?] conditions of the [? war ?] [? resistance. ?] And TB-- at the end of July, she passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she when she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she died, she was around 30 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- you were telling me that you-- then you went to Berlin. What did you do in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin? You see-- [LAUGHS] you have to-- we are-- we are in the Russian zone. We are under the Russians. Now, I have to go to the-- have to go to the west-- to the west. I have to reach either American zone or the English zone. We are in Berlin, surrounded by the Russians. But Berlin had three zones or four zones-- Russian zone, English zone, French zone, and US-- United States zone. So we came to the east zone. We smuggled ourselves by-- by [? throwing ?] to the west

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Dr. Kronzon. He already is married. He has a wife, also a doctor. He found a-- a relative-- we'll say relative-- a nephew, and we went to the American zone. There  the United Nations Relief Organization takes over, took us over-- very fine, relatively fine conditions, clean rooms, good food, you know, et cetera. And we're to wait-- to wait for a-- for a truck which will bring us to the west zone. And it was approximately one week we had to wait, and then two or three trucks full with either Jews or with Poles or with others who want to go to the west side. And we reported to Hanover. It's a city already in western Germany. I had to go to Eckernforde. That is north-- north of Germany. My friend, Kronzon says, I'm going south to-- to Munich, because he knew that there is accumulation of Jewish, which was seen in Munich. And-- and we parted. I found that my wife is dead. I went also to Munich. In Munich, I found many friends who were in a. [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I couldn't stand the life there in St. Ottilien It was a [? Jew ?] hospital-- hospital where they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the name again.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the  name of the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: St. Ottilien. It is a big monastery. The Germans converted this monastery into a military hospital. And after the Americans came and occupied, they going to give this hospital to the Jews to bring your-- your-- your sick, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked [? there, ?] [? too, ?] yes. Not very long, because the supply of doctors was relatively enough, and I went to Munich. I went to Munich. I went to the University Hospital of Munich, to the obstetrical department asking permission to come to see. But I got very, very-- right away you can [? come. ?] After all, now I am a liberated Jew in Germany. It's a completely different situation. After three months I am already a full-time employee in this hospital. And soon, I am already employed with a patient. And I became a part of this hospital, where I spent five years.

speaker: interviewee
text: From this hospital, I published 11 scientific papers. On what subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight or nine were [? object ?] all connected with concentration camp-related effects, and then the related-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all from this-- from this-- the-- I knew that I have not enough material. I have only 1,500 cases. I've only around 80-- 80 malformed very high-risk abnormalities.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about abnormalities of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of babies born? Of babies born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, babies born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of illnesses where they born with? What kind of abnormality?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most frequent was mongoloids.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were of women who had been through the concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. Mostly mongoloids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else? What other abnormality?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other is what are called central nervous system.

speaker: interviewer
text: For instance what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Central nervous system.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hydrocephalus, a lot of water; anencephelus, without the brain; microcephaly, small brain; spina bifida, the division of the spine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And forgive me for asking you to describe it, briefly, but what did you attribute those illnesses to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To--

speaker: interviewer
text: Those disorders.

speaker: interviewee
text: --severe hunger and emotional stress of the mother, which affected her ovaries, because they were liberated. Please, I am talking about women who are already over a year liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're-- was it the emotional stress alone, or was it coupled with the hunger?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't divide, but being in concentration camp, knowing what most affected, I said, it is not hunger. It is emotion stress which kills more people than hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you felt that the emotional stress could have caused the mongoloid, is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through damage-- damage of the ovaries, and damage of the ovaries, and damage of the gametes, which is in the ovaries.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would emotional illness affect ovaries?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear, my dear, it is simple. It could-- could affect, directly, the ovaries. If a woman is suddenly under severe emotional stress, she may stop menstruating. She may stop menstruating, and that is, if you want to ask why, because the ovaries are affected. The ovaries are not producing, working normally, and you can expect everything [? that you want ?] possible-- maybe miscarriages, maybe malformations, et cetera. Nobody was talking. I was talking  about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to know, what did you do with those babies that we're born with these illnesses? Where they kept alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure-- kept alive. I kept alive, and the Jewish committee even established a special place for all these malformed children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I will not say where it was. Dr. Priskin, who was the director of all this told me, because I presented the results of these studies to-- at a convention of Jewish doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? No, no, no. Sit down. Sit down. No, you can't move. No, no, no. Go back. Go back. You'll tell me later.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, fine. When was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: The doctor-- one second. [INTERPOSING VOICES] I just want to clarify. One second.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was somewhere beginning 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: One second. I just want to clarify. The doctor was getting up in order to show me something, but you'll show it to me later on during our break.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't move around now during the interview. You'll show me the book later. I want to ask you something. Was it customary for a woman with a child with a physical disorder or emotional disorder to take that child and raise that child in her own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she could. She could. Nobody compelled her. But you must understand that these women-- if a child had, we'll say, some other handicaps-- I will give you an example. And nobody wants to stay in Germany. Some are going to South Africa. Some are going to Palestine. Some are going to Canada, Australia-- Canada, a few. The United States, too, was closed, until the Act of Displaced Persons came out, where they allowed 300,000 displaced persons to come to the United States. But it is not Jewish. It is mostly Poles, mostly Russians, mostly Czechs, all from the east countries. We didn't want to go back-- back to-- to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could these women take into America or into these other countries deformed children?

speaker: interviewee
text: That will not-- the Americans will not allow.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was  not permitted?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not allowed. If you want in, either you live here, or you stay here. Stay here. Very many stayed with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who assess the baby's health in order to determine--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, one second. Who assessed, who evaluated the baby's health in order to determine--

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear--

speaker: interviewer
text: --whether that baby was healthy or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they had their-- their doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: The American government?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, also from a liberated-- liberated. One, for example, Doctor Herman-- he was a very good surgeon, but he worked at that time just checking all these things. You know, people were going to the United States. He had to check their health.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to ask you now, was it-- was it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was it common practice for a doctor upon delivering a deformed child to kill the child at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: In spite of the physical or obvious deformity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Nobody-- nobody kills-- kills. If you kill, you killed. It is an old crime. We are not allowed, as doctors, to kill abnormal children.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked at the University Hospital in Munich for four or five years. I published a lot of scientific papers, all connected, mostly connected, with the concentration camp with a very strong response from other countries, even the Germans, and whatever. Finally, the Russians published their result, all confirming my findings. I introduced that it is not nor usually acceptable to do [INAUDIBLE] if you damage an embryo already-- or you-- if you deliver, you didn't have-- naturally, a surgeon, it could be that you have inherited such a thing. I introduced the term that it is plasmatic damage of the egg, that the plasma of the egg is damaged. I couldn't define what it is. I could define, but it is the plasma of the egg is damaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: The plasma of the egg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The-- the nucleus and the-- that was damaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hunger and severe emotional distress. You cannot relate to this, but it is hunger and severe emotional distress. It's accepted all over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- the-- hold on. Four years after I published my papers, a paper appears in Sweden, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where they studied, now, the cells-- ovarian cells, we'll say-- cells, the gametes. First, they said it is not 48 chromosomes, but 46 chromosomes. And then they segregated in a very fine way-- these two America scientists, Tjio and Levan, they did these studies in Sweden at the University of Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they find?

speaker: interviewee
text: Found? They didn't find. [INAUDIBLE] They found that-- they first went into the how a cell is built. We had-- we knew already it is 48 chromosomes, but they-- they did a beautiful job. For me, they deserve to have a Nobel Prize.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I need to ask you now--

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, you know, when this was established, now, in England, they started to study hormonal deficiencies in the babies. Maybe it is connected with the chromosomal status. And the first [? full ?] did it on-- because most frequent-- in my papers was most frequent-- were mongoloids. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Lejeune. What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lejeune is the name of a scientist from-- in Paris. He started to study the chromosomes in mongoloids. And here he found, you know, that instead of 46 chromosomes, they have 45 chromosomes. One chromosome is missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you to continue, though, to tell me about your practice in America. When did you actually arrive in America?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, with just one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had you married? Who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married a woman-- a woman who I got acquainted with in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Galina Raicinska.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after your wife had died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure after my wife. It was three-- it was almost four years after she died. No, no-- three years after my wife died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was-- you came to America with her with the child? What kind of child-- boy or girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the child's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anatol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the University Hospital where I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to America where, did-- well, let me ask. Why did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear, I will-- I will give you, if you want, I will give you a wider answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one sentence. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I came to my professor, who was very good to me, I will say. And I told him, Herr professor, I decided I am leaving Germany. He was a 72-year-old, 73-year-old man. I am already 88, so [INAUDIBLE] I considered him very old. He jumps up in German, and he says,

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] --and you too. You are leaving Germany. Why? You are now an associate professor. In two years, you're a full professor, and then you get either a-- a chairmanship of the hospital, or a big hospital management. You are making a career. Why are you going away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I know I will have here very well. My son probably will have it well, too. But you killed, in the 11th century, Jews because of Crusades. Whole towns-- towns were destroyed by the crusaders when they were going [INAUDIBLE]. In the 13th and 14th centuries, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews were killed. In Europe, was terrible epidemics, accusing that the Jews brought it. Again, the Jews were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 15th century, already gone were the epidemics. Germany is divided into small principalities, barons and graf, and a Jewish merchant will come to sell his merchandise and pay taxes. And such a baron or graf will say, why I have to-- to take taxes? I will kill him, and then I will take the whole merchandise. And that was, I say, in the 15th century.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 30 years, Protestants were fighting the Catholics. 1618, 1614.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then still, you know, I said to him German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [? And ?] Jewish [INAUDIBLE]. And now, what I went through here. Ergo, you are a country here of physical anti-Semites. [? Ergo ?] [INAUDIBLE] to come here back. Not one Jew, in my opinion, has to stay in Germany. Neither in Germany, nor in Poland, nor in Hungary, nor in Romania-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all countries with physical anti-Semites. He looked at me and didn't say a word. We remained best friends for 12 years. He came even with his wife to New York. He stayed in my apartment for almost two weeks, although he had to go to his daughter, who was married to an American doctor-- she's a doctor, and the American doctor-- in and out of town, Washington, Pennsylvania. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Whenever I was quite frequently going to Europe, every two years, making a trip. I was making-- I made very soon a career, you know, here in the United States, working at Mt. Sinai, having and developing a nice practice.

speaker: interviewer
text: In obstetrics?

speaker: interviewee
text: In obstetrics and gynecology. I already applied-- I didn't have to take-- for specialty, now. [INAUDIBLE] I had to get my specialty. I was permitted to take-- because of my publications of my previous studies in Munich, I was-- didn't have to go as a resident, just simply to take the examinations. And in 1960 I protracted. And in 1960 I became a diplomat, American-style.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long did you practice obstetrics and gynecology in New York? Until what year? [INAUDIBLE] life, I was in New York-- all [? the ?] life. It is from '53 to '70-- to '80. [INAUDIBLE]. So if I were to ask you now, how many babies did you personally deliver?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately, I told you. Approximately, it must be-- it stretches-- stretches around 40 years or 50 years of practice, you know. So I counted [INAUDIBLE] approximately it should be around 7,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: 7,000 Babies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine-- I delivered. But I was-- in [INAUDIBLE], I was the director. I was before head of obstetrics for a year in the hospital, University Hospital. I delivered there Jewish women. [? Maternity ?] [? duty ?] other, you know-- it doesn't matter. Over 3,000 under my supervision. I am the responsible person. And then, you know, together it would be around 20,000 under my supervision.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in touch with any of those babies that you brought into the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [? why am I ?] [? attached? ?] You know, I see this baby for, basically, a delivery, maybe one day later on, you know. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you proud of being an obstetrician, gynecologist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, proud? Yes. It was a very good specialty until malpractice started. The most vulnerable specialty, which became in danger, very much, it was obstetrics. And I said, you know, that in the 15th, 16th, 17th century, a woman gave birth to an abnormal child, and if the husband wanted to get rid of her, he would say she had relations with the devil. That was enough. She would be burned at stake. Now, the new devil is not the devil. It's the doctor. When every woman delivers an abnormal child, the doctor's responsible. And I said when I saw this, and I was hit with this, I said, that's enough. This nonsense I don't want to put up with anymore. And I'm sorry. It was on my 80th birthday on November 1, 1887--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1987. I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you practicing medicine at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: 80 Is a pretty long career.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is not-- 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 50 years is a very long career.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after? I want to ask you first, what happened to your second wife? Did you have more children with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second wife. My second wife, shortly, will say, in '67, she was officially 49, but I know that she made herself younger, because after liberation she didn't have any papers. She could say, I'm born on this day and this day. And she's 53-- got a severe heart attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: She died?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 10 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was in New York? You were living in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, on West End Avenue.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you have with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two-- two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the other child's name? The child that was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrew Mark Klebanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was born in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in America at Mt. Sinai where I work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage with two young children by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a very, very big problem. I was having-- I had at that time an excellent practice. I had to bring-- you know, I had to-- it was a problem. One son was 17 years old. The other was 13 years old. So it was not babies. They were already grown up, that to manage them, yes, it was a hard time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a failed help, that was the sister of my late wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: That one didn't work out well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other one?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, simply-- simply I had the women, you know, working at home [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every day. So [? dishonest, ?] they would go to school, and from school, you know, I am still keeping an eye on them. They didn't need another. And in 1970, I married my second wife-- my third wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your third wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sophie.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to give you a chance to introduce her now. We're going to wait a minute, and we're going to introduce Sophie.
